text: The MSRE first attained criticality on June 1, 1965 with a $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration within $1\%$ of the predicted value.

Initial critical conditions were $1181^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , fuel circulation stopped, one control rod inserted 0.03 of its worth and $65.4\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the loop (plus $4.2\mathrm{kg}$ in a drain tank).

Subsequently the reactor was held at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while the $^{235}\mathrm{UF}_4$ concentration was increased by the addition of 79 enriching capsules containing $6.6\mathrm{kg}$ $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .

During these additions, experiments were done to measure rod sensitivity and total worth, $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration coefficient of reactivity, temperature coefficient, pressure coefficient, and effect of circulation on reactivity.
Nuclear power was restricted to a nominal 10 watts, except during transients, when 10 kw was permitted.
The experiment was concluded on July 3 and the salt was drained to permit preparations for high-power operation.
Preliminary testing of the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment began in the fall of 1964.
The salt systems were heated to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , purged with helium to remove moisture, and 66 LiF-34 $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ salt was charged into the fuel and coolant systems.
Salt was circulated for 1000 hr in the fuel system and 1200 hr in the coolant system as most of the non-nuclear testing was completed.
This run, designated PC-1, was concluded in March, 1965.
The fuel system was heated and this "carrier" salt was circulated for 10 days in Run PC-2.
Addition of $^{235}\mathrm{UF}_4$ -LiF to the salt began on May 24, and on June 1 criticality was first attained.
Following this experiment, more $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added to bring the concentration up to the level required for power operation.
While the extra $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was being added, experiments were done to measure the nuclear characteristics of the system.
This series of experiments was completed on July 3, the fuel salt was drained and the system was flushed, concluding Run 3.
It also served to establish the basepoint from which the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ additions necessary to reach the operating concentration could be made with confidence.
The fuel salt composition specified for power operation is 65 LiF-29.2 BeF $_2$ -5 ZrF $_4$ -0.8 UF $_4$ (expressed as molar percentages).

The critical $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration was predicted by calculations using a multi-group, one-dimensional diffusion code, MODRIC, with thermal-group cross sections obtained from cell calculations by the THERMOS code and fast-group cross sections calculated by GAM-2. $^{1,2}$ The geometrical approximations were checked by using a two-group, two-dimensional code, Equi-poise-3, with group constants for each region from MODRIC.
It was predicted that the reactor would be critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , rods out, fuel static with 0.256 mole $\%$ $^{235}\mathrm{UF}_4$ (0.795 mole $\%$ total $\mathrm{UF}_4$ ).

Instead of using $32\%$ -enriched uranium to make up the fuel salt, we decided to start with depleted uranium in the salt and add the required amount of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ as highly enriched uranium $(93\% ^{235}\mathrm{U})$ .

The salt was prepared in three lots: the carriers salt, containing the beryllium, zirconium and most of the lithium fluorides; 73 LiF-27 $\mathrm{UF_4}$ eutectic containing $150~\mathrm{kg}$ of depleted uranium; and eutectic containing $90~\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the highly enriched form.
This mixture of salt was then circulated for 10 days at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while the sampler-enricher was tested and 18 samples were analyzed to
Nuclear instrumentation for the experiment consisted of two fission chambers, two $\mathrm{BF}_3$ chambers and an $^{241}\mathrm{Am}-^{242}\mathrm{Cm}$ -Be source, located as shown in Fig.
The fuel salt itself also constituted a neutron source, due to reaction of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ alphas with beryllium and fluorine.
The latter method was limited to $85\mathrm{g}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ per capsule, only 0.0012 of the expected critical loading.
Therefore the bulk of the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added in 4 additions to the drain tank.
After the third addition, with $64.54\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the salt, the projected critical loading was $70.0 \pm 0.5\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .
$1\mathrm{kg}$ below the critical point.

No question of safety was involved if there had been an overshoot.) $4.38\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added and the count rates showed the loading was within $0.8\mathrm{kg}$ of critical when the rods were withdrawn and circulation was stopped.
With the reactor within $0.2\%$ $\delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ of critical, slight variations in temperature caused considerable changes in multiplication.

(Variations in the voltage of the area power supply change the heater inputs slightly, requiring fine adjustments of the heater controls to keep the temperature precisely at a specified temperature.) After 7 capsules, it appeared that after one more, the reactor could be made critical.
At 6:00 PM, June 1, the reactor reached the critical point, with two rods at full withdrawal and the other inserted 0.03 of its worth.
The $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading was $69.6~\mathrm{kg}$ .
The "observed" $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentrations are on a weight basis, obtained from either inventory records or from chemical analyses.
The amounts of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and salt weighed into the system gave a $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ weight fraction of $1.42\%$ at the time of the initial criticality.
The core temperature was $1181^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ instead of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the control rods were poisoning $0.184\%$ $\mathrm{8k / k}$ instead of none.

(Two rods were at maximum withdrawal, 51 inches, and one was at 46.6 in.) The predicted $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration for criticality at the reference condition was $32.87~\mathrm{g / l}$ ; corrected to the actual conditions, it is $33.06~\mathrm{g / l}$ .
This predicted value is compared with "observed" concentrations in Table 1.
-Table1 COMPARISON OF CRITICAL 235U CONCENTRATIONS 1181°F, PUMP OFF, 0.18% 8k/k ROD POISONING

The addition of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ beyond the minimum critical loading had a twofold objective: to end with enough excess reactivity to permit operation at full power and in the process to make measurements which could be analyzed to give control-rod worth and various reactivity coefficients.
The final amount of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was to be enough to be critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the fuel stationary and one rod fully inserted.
The method was to add $85\mathrm{g}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ at a time through the sampler-enricher, after each capsule determine the critical rod position, and at longer intervals do other experiments.

After the initial critical experiment, another 8 capsules were required before the reactor could be made critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the fuel pump running (a consequence of the loss of delayed neutrons during circulation).
At intervals of 4 capsules, we made period
This went on until a total of 87 capsules had been added.
Three times during this experiment (after 30, 65, and 87 capsules) we observed rod-drop effects.

The rod whose sensitivity was to be measured was adjusted to make the reactor just critical at approximately 10 watts, then it was pulled a prescribed distance and held there until the power increased about 2 decades.
The rod was then quickly inserted to bring the power back to 10 watts and the measurement was repeated at a shorter stable period.
Periods were generally in the ranges from 40 to 50 sec and from 70 to 120 sec.

(The stable period was taken to be the average of their two readings, which usually agreed within about $2\%$ .) We watched a linear recorder, driven by a compensated ion chamber, while we leveled the power at the beginning of a measurement.
In the analysis, we computed the initial reactivity (usually less than $0.003\%$ ) from this recorder chart if there had been a slow drift.

Because rod worth is affected by the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration in the core, it was necessary to apply theoretical corrections to the measured sensitivities to put them all on the basis of one concentration.
4 were corrected to the initial critical concentration, where the sensitivity is the highest.
The correction factors which were applied increase linearly with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration to a maximum of 1.03 at the final concentration (the points between 1 and 2 inch).
Figure 5 shows a curve of rod effect vs position at the initial concentration which is the integral of the differential-worth curve in Fig.
The curve for the final concentration is simply the first curve reduced by a factor of 1.03.

The change in the static multiplication constant with all three rods inserted all the way through the MSRE core, and with the fuel composition practically the same as the initial experimental composition, was $-5.51\%$ in $\mathrm{ke}$ .
This is equivalent to a static reactivity of $-5.79\% \Delta \mathrm{k_e} / \mathrm{k_e}$ .
For these same conditions, this theoretical model gave a static reactivity of $-2.28\% \Delta \mathrm{k_e} / \mathrm{k_e}$ for rod 2 with rods 1 and 3 completely removed.
Although the actual rod calibrated was rod 1, complete calculations were made with
The worth of the partially inserted, banked rods was calculated for a cylindrical annular model of the rod bank, using the two-group, two-dimensional program, Equipoise-3.
These results indicated that the available travel of 51 in. covered 0.927 of the worth of rods all the way through the core.
(The effective height of the core, extending into the upper and lower heads, is 78.2 in.) Thus the effective static reactivity for rod 2 was $-2.28 \times .927 = 2.11\%$ .

Earlier calculations made for different fuel salt compositions had indicated that the worth of either rod 1 or rod 3 is about 1.03 times that for rod 2, due to the positions of the rods relative to the graphite sample holder.
Thus the predicted worth of rod 1 would be about $2.2\%$ , in favorable agreement with the experimental measurements.

The $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration coefficient of reactivity is given by the ratio of the change in reactivity to the fractional change in $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration (or circulating mass) as a result of a small addition.
5 and correcting for the concentration effect on rod worth.
6 at any concentration multiplied by that concentration is the desired concentration coefficient of reactivity, $(\delta k / k) / (\delta m / m)$ .
The coefficients obtained in this way range from 0.22 at the final concentration to 0.23 at the lowest concentration.
The coefficient predicted from multigroup criticality searches about the minimum critical concentration was 0.248.
6, is $-0.212 \pm 0.004\% \delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ .
The effect of changes in delayed-neutron precursor distributions with circulation had been predicted to be $-0.30\% \delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ .
Another $-0.2\% \delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ was expected because of entrained bubbles of helium in the circulating salt.

We measured the effect of temperature on reactivity by adjusting the electric heaters to change the system temperature slowly (about $15^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ per hour) while we observed the critical position of the regulating rod.
Figure 7 shows results of the three experiments involving slow changes in temperature.
The first experiment, with $64\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in circulation, gave a line whose slope ranges from $-(6.6$ to $8.3)\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
At $66\mathrm{kg}$ the experiment gave a straight line with a slope of $-7.24\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
In the last experiment, at $68\mathrm{kg}$ , the slope of the curve above about $1140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is $-7.3\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
A value of $-7.0\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ had been predicted: $-3.3\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ for the fuel and $-3.7\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ for the graphite.
Recent measurements of fuel-salt density gave a higher thermal-expansion coefficient, leading to a calculated fuel temperature coefficient of reactivity of $-5.6\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
The new value for overall temperature coefficient is $-9.6\times 10^{-5}\circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
The experiment at $68\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ shows a lower slope below about $1140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The output of a thermocouple in the reactor-vessel outlet, logged digitally at $1/4$ -sec intervals, showed a brief increase of 5 to $6^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ as the hot salt first passed.
It then leveled at about $3.5^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for a few loop transit times before decreasing gradually.

The noise in the analog-to-digital conversion ( $\pm 1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) limited the accuracy of the measurement, but by taking an average of 50 points during the level period after mixing and before the graphite temperature had time to change significantly, a value was obtained for the step in fuel temperature.
The result was a change of $-(4.9 \pm 2.3) \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~F}^{-1}$ .

In each of the three tests the loop overpressure was slowly increased from the normal 5 psig to 10 to 15 psig and then quickly relieved, through a bypass valve, to a drain tank that had been previously vented to atmospheric pressure.
The third test was performed at an abnormally low pump-bowl level which was obtained by lowering the operating temperature to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Figure 8 shows the pressure transient and the responses of the regulating control rod, densitometer, and fuel-pump level during the rapid pressure release.
Independent evaluations of the void fraction from these three parameters all gave values between 2 and 3 percent by volume.
9 along with the predicted high-frequency response for a void fraction of $1.2\%$ .
Extrapolation to the observed curve gives a void fraction of 2 to 2-1/2 percent.
The low- and high-frequency pressure coefficients were + 0.0003 and + 0.014(\% $\delta$ k/k)/psi, respectively, for this particular condition.
Procedures -- In the pulse tests a control rod was withdrawn 1/2 in., held there for 3-1/2 or 7 sec., then returned to its original position.
The rod was placed so that this rod motion caused a change in $\mathrm{k_{eff}}$ of about $0.03\%$ .
The rod-position signal and flux signal were recorded digitally at 0.25-sec intervals using the MSRE on-line digital computer (referred to in Figs.
The series contained 19 bits each with a duration of 7 sec., and was repeated several times so that the initial transients could die out.
The rod motion about the average position corresponded to a $\Delta k_{\mathrm{eff}}$ of about $\pm 0.0015\%$ .
The rod-position signal and flux-level signal were recorded digitally at 0.25-sec intervals using the MSRE computer.
These tests were hampered by the unfavorable location of the detector and the resulting low flux-signal level at low power (~10 watts).
10 through 14 along with theoretical predictions for the runs with the fuel pump on and with the pump off.

Thus, the noise-analysis data were arbitrarily multiplied by the factor required to give agreement with theoretical results at a frequency of 9 radians/sec.
Figure 15 shows the noise analysis results for operation at a reduced pump-bowl level, which caused increased bubble entrainment in the fuel salt.
15) and the noise spectrum obtained with no bubbles circulating (Fig.
10) indicates that the bubbles increased the amplitude of the power spectral density significantly in the 1- to 10-rad/sec region.
Figures 16 and 17 show the fuel-pump speed, coolant-pump speed, and coolant-salt flow rate vs time for pump startup and cooldown.
Fuel samples were removed daily from the pump bowl during the last precritical operation (Run PC-2) and the zero-power experiments (Run 3).

The analyses support the conclusions that the salt was given excellent protection against moisture or oxygen, that no uranium precipitated, and that essentially no corrosion of the INOR-8 in the fuel system occurred during the period of approximately 1100 hours.
Two of the control rods and drives were installed during run PC-1 and were used in simulator training.
Before PC-2 all three rods were installed and subjected to a test consisting of 100 cycles of full withdrawal and scram.
After we installed stellite bearing strips to remedy this problem, each rod was successfully raised and scrambled 30 times without any malfunction.
(The lower limit switch on Rod 2 at first stuck as before, and we found that a shim had been left out of the
Rod drop times were measured in the tests in PC-2 and in a series of 40 scams at the end of Run 3.
The results (Table 3) show that the drop times became slightly shorter and more uniform.
a Time from actuation of the scram switch (with rods at 5l-in. withdrawal) until actuation of lower limit switch (0 inch).
Several problems occurred with the freeze valves, but most of these were corrected prior to Run PC-2.
During critical operation the only important requirement is that of ensuring a sufficiently short thaw time for the drain valve, FV-103.
A proportional controller was added to the FV-103 cooling-air supply to maintain the freeze valve at a preselected temperature which would result in a suitable thaw time.
An incident occurred during Run 3 which could have delayed an emergency drain of the fuel system had it been required.
Electrical power to the freeze-valve control modules was lost for a period of about 10 min because the terminals of a temporary recorder were accidentally shorted.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: During Run PC-1 (January - March, 1965), when the coolant salt was circulated for 1200 hours, the coolant offgas filter plugged and was replaced twice.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: When the filters plugged, pressure was controlled at $5 \pm 2$ psig by manual venting through a larger bypass valve.) Inspection showed that the filter was covered with amorphous carbon containing traces of the constituents of the coolant salt and INOR-8.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: The filter was replaced before PC-2 with one having 35 times the surface area.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: Coolant salt was not circulated again until near the end of Run 3 and then for only 118 hours.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: The fuel-system pressure control became erratic near the end of Run PC-1 and at the conclusion of that run the offgas filter was removed.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: The acetone rinse was darkened and contained small $(1 - 5\mu)$ beads of a glassy substance.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: Fuel- system pressure control was satisfactory at the beginning of Run PC-2, but within a week the valve began sticking again.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: This time the valve was replaced with one having a larger $C_{\mathrm{v}}$ (0.077 instead of 0.02).

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: The deposit was about $20\%$ amorphous carbon and the remainder was the $1 - 5\mu$ glassy beads, which proved to have the composition of the flush salt.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: The larger replacement valve in the fuel offgas line gave adequate pressure control until near the end of Run 3, after about 30 days of salt circulation, when it appeared to be sticking occasionally.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: When this happened, the pressure built up slowly to about 6 psig before the valve opened to drop it back to the normal 5 psig.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: A filter, capable of removing $1 - \mu$ particles, is to be installed in the fuel system.

section: Fuel and Coolant System Pressure Control
text: (The pore size of the original filter was about $25\mu$ .) Presumably this will protect the valve from further accumulations.
